verses.doctrinal_note: John's confrontation exposes the danger of superficial religiosity and hypocrisy. True faith requires genuine repentance and a transformed heart, not merely outward observance or association with religious groups, especially when attempting to evade divine wrath.

verses.kjv_text: Bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance:
verses.original_text: ποιήσατε οὖν καρπὸν ἄξιον τῆς μετανοίας·
verses.transliteration: poiēsate oun karpon axion tēs metanoias;
verses.study_note: John demands “fruits meet for repentance” (καρπὸν ἄξιον τῆς μετανοίας), emphasizing that true repentance is not just an internal feeling but must be demonstrated by outward actions and a changed lifestyle. This concept of 'fruit' is central to biblical ethics, indicating that genuine faith produces tangible evidence (cf. Matt 7:16-20; Gal 5:22-23).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse highlights the inseparable link between genuine repentance and ethical conduct. Authentic faith is not passive but active, producing a life that reflects God's righteousness and demonstrates a true turning away from sin.

verses.kjv_text: And think not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, that God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham.
verses.original_text: καὶ μὴ δόξητε λέγειν ἐν ἑαυτοῖς, Πατέρα ἔχομεν τὸν Ἀβραάμ· λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν ὅτι δύναται ὁ Θεὸς ἐκ τῶν λίθων τούτων ἐγεῖραι τέκνα τῷ Ἀβραάμ.
verses.transliteration: kai mē doxēte legein en heautois, Patera echomen ton Abraam; legō gar hymin hoti dynatai ho Theos ek tōn lithōn toutōn egeirai tekna tō Abraam.
verses.study_note: John challenges the Pharisees' reliance on their Abrahamic lineage for salvation. He asserts that God is not limited by human ancestry and can “raise up children unto Abraham” from stones, emphasizing God's sovereign power and the spiritual nature of true sonship (cf. Rom 9:6-8; Gal 3:7). Physical descent from Abraham was insufficient without spiritual transformation.
verses.doctrinal_note: This statement refutes the notion of inherited salvation or ethnic privilege before God. It underscores that God's covenant promises are fulfilled through faith and spiritual lineage, not merely biological descent, emphasizing the universal call to repentance and faith.

verses.kjv_text: And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: therefore every tree which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire.
verses.original_text: ἤδη δὲ ἡ ἀξίνη πρὸς τὴν ῥίζαν τῶν δένδρων κεῖται· πᾶν οὖν δένδρον μὴ ποιοῦν καρπὸν καλὸν ἐκκόπτεται καὶ εἰς πῦρ βάλλεται.
verses.transliteration: ēdē de hē axinē pros tēn rhizan tōn dendrōn keitai; pan oun dendron mē poioun karpon kalon ekkoptetai kai eis pyr balletai.
verses.study_note: The imagery of the “axe laid unto the root of the trees” signifies imminent divine judgment. Just as a fruitless tree is cut down and burned, so too will those who do not produce “good fruit” (i.e., genuine repentance and righteousness) face destruction. This serves as a stark warning to those who resist John's call.
verses.doctrinal_note: This prophetic warning illustrates the certainty and severity of divine judgment for those who fail to bear the fruit of repentance. It emphasizes that God's patience has limits and that a day of reckoning awaits all who reject His call to transformation.

verses.kjv_text: I indeed baptize you with water unto repentance: but he that cometh after me is mightier than I, whose shoes I am not worthy to bear: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire:
verses.original_text: Ἐγὼ μὲν ὑμᾶς βαπτίζω ἐν ὕδατι εἰς μετάνοιαν· ὁ δὲ ὀπίσω μου ἐρχόμενος ἰσχυρότερός μού ἐστιν, οὗ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἱκανὸς τὰ ὑποδήματα βαστάσαι· αὐτὸς ὑμᾶς βαπτίσει ἐν Πνεύματι Ἁγίῳ καὶ πυρί.
verses.transliteration: Egō men hymas baptizō en hydati eis metanoian; ho de opisō mou erchomenos ischyroteros mou estin, hou ouk eimi hikanos ta hypodēmata bastasai; autos hymas baptisei en Pneumati Hagiō kai pyri.
verses.study_note: John contrasts his water baptism, which symbolizes repentance, with the future baptism by the “mightier one” (Jesus) with the “Holy Ghost, and with fire.” This dual baptism refers to the empowering presence of the Spirit and the purifying, refining, or judgmental work of God (cf. Acts 1:5; 2:3-4). John humbly acknowledges his inferiority, unworthy even to perform a servant's task.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse distinguishes John's preparatory baptism from the transformative work of Christ. Jesus' baptism with the Holy Spirit empowers believers for service and sanctification, while the "fire" aspect can signify either purification for the faithful or judgment for the unrepentant.

verses.kjv_text: Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and gather his wheat into the garner; but he will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire.
verses.original_text: οὗ τὸ πτύον ἐν τῇ χειρὶ αὐτοῦ, καὶ διακαθαριεῖ τὴν ἅλωνα αὐτοῦ, καὶ συνάξει τὸν σῖτον αὐτοῦ εἰς τὴν ἀποθήκην, τὸ δὲ ἄχυρον κατακαύσει πυρὶ ἀσβέστῳ.
verses.transliteration: hou to ptyon en tē cheiri autou, kai diakathariei tēn halōna autou, kai synaxei ton siton autou eis tēn apothēkēn, to de achyron katakausei pyri asbestō.
verses.study_note: John continues the imagery of judgment, portraying Jesus as a farmer separating wheat from chaff. The “fan” (πτύον, ptyon, a winnowing fork) is used to toss the grain into the air, allowing the wind to blow away the light chaff while the heavier wheat falls to the threshing floor. The wheat (the righteous) will be gathered into the “garner” (ἀποθήκην, apothēkēn, barn), while the chaff (the unrighteous) will be burned with “unquenchable fire” (πυρὶ ἀσβέστῳ, pyri asbestō), symbolizing eternal judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: This vivid imagery powerfully depicts Christ's role as the ultimate judge, separating the righteous from the wicked. It underscores the finality of divine judgment and the eternal consequences for those who reject God's grace and fail to align their lives with His will.

verses.kjv_text: Then cometh Jesus from Galilee to Jordan unto John, to be baptized of him.
verses.original_text: Τότε παραγίνεται ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἀπὸ τῆς Γαλιλαίας ἐπὶ τὸν Ἰορδάνην πρὸς τὸν Ἰωάννην τοῦ βαπτισθῆναι ὑπ’ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Tote paragineta ho Iēsous apo tēs Galilaias epi ton Iordanēn pros ton Iōannēn tou baptisthēnai hyp’ autou.
verses.study_note: Jesus' arrival from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John marks a significant transition. It signifies the beginning of His public ministry and His identification with humanity, even though He was without sin. This act was a deliberate step in God's redemptive plan.

verses.kjv_text: But John forbad him, saying, I have need to be baptized of thee, and comest thou to me?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰωάννης διεκώλυεν αὐτὸν λέγων, Ἐγὼ χρείαν ἔχω ὑπὸ σοῦ βαπτισθῆναι, καὶ σὺ ἔρχῃ πρός με;
verses.transliteration: ho de Iōannēs diekōlyen auton legōn, Egō chreian echō hypo sou baptisthēnai, kai sy erchē pros me?
verses.study_note: John's initial reluctance (“forbad him”) highlights his recognition of Jesus' superior status and his own unworthiness. He understood that Jesus, being sinless, did not need a baptism of repentance. John's statement, “I have need to be baptized of thee,” acknowledges Jesus' divine authority and purifying power.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering said unto him, Suffer it to be so now: for thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness. Then he suffered him.
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτόν, Ἄφες ἄρτι· οὕτως γὰρ πρέπον ἐστὶν ἡμῖν πληρῶσαι πᾶσαν δικαιοσύνην. τότε ἀφίησιν αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen pros auton, Aphes arti; houtōs gar prepon estin hēmin plērōsai pasan dikaiosynēn. tote aphiēsin auton.
verses.study_note: Jesus' response, “Suffer it to be so now: for thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness,” is crucial. His baptism was not for His own repentance but to identify with humanity, validate John's ministry, and inaugurate His messianic work in obedience to God's will. It was an act of solidarity and submission to the divine plan, setting an example of perfect obedience.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' baptism, though not for His own sin, was essential for "fulfilling all righteousness." It signifies His perfect obedience to God's will, His identification with humanity's need for cleansing, and His inauguration as the Messiah who would bear the sins of the world.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus, when he was baptized, went up straightway out of the water: and, lo, the heavens were opened unto him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove, and lighting upon him:
verses.original_text: βαπτισθεὶς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εὐθὺς ἀνέβη ἀπὸ τοῦ ὕδατος· καὶ ἰδοὺ ἠνεῴχθησαν οἱ οὐρανοί, καὶ εἶδεν [τὸ] Πνεῦμα [τοῦ] Θεοῦ καταβαῖνον ὡσεὶ περιστερὰν ἐρχόμενον ἐπ’ αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: baptistheis de ho Iēsous euthys anebē apo tou hydatos; kai idou ēneōchthēsan hoi ouranoi, kai eiden [to] Pneuma [tou] Theou katabainon hōsei peristeran erchomenon ep’ auton.
verses.study_note: Immediately after Jesus' baptism, the heavens were opened, and the Spirit of God descended “like a dove” and lit upon Him. This visible manifestation of the Holy Spirit confirms Jesus' divine anointing for His messianic mission. The dove symbolizes purity, peace, and the presence of God (cf. Gen 8:8-12). This event marks the Trinitarian nature of God at work.
verses.doctrinal_note: The descent of the Holy Spirit upon Jesus in the form of a dove signifies His divine anointing and empowerment for His messianic ministry. This event, alongside the Father's voice, provides a clear manifestation of the Trinity—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—working in perfect unity.

verses.kjv_text: And lo a voice from heaven, saying, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ φωνὴ ἐκ τῶν οὐρανῶν λέγουσα, Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ Υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός, ἐν ᾧ εὐδόκησα.
verses.transliteration: kai idou phōnē ek tōn ouranōn legousa, Houtos estin ho Huios mou ho agapētos, en hō eudokēsa.
verses.study_note: The voice from heaven, “This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased,” is a direct divine affirmation of Jesus' identity and mission. This echoes Psalm 2:7 (“Thou art my Son”) and Isaiah 42:1 (“my chosen one in whom my soul delights”), linking Jesus to both the Davidic Messiah and the Suffering Servant. This declaration publicly validates Jesus as the Son of God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Father's declaration from heaven unequivocally affirms Jesus' divine Sonship and His unique relationship with God. This public endorsement establishes Jesus' authority and identity as the Messiah, the beloved Son in whom God finds ultimate pleasure, initiating His redemptive work.

2.meaning: Imperative form of `μετανοέω`, meaning 'to change one's mind, to repent'. It denotes a fundamental transformation of one's inner disposition, leading to a turning away from sin and a reorientation towards God. It's more than regret; it's a decisive shift in allegiance and lifestyle.

Refers to kingship, royal power, or kingdom. In the phrase "kingdom of heaven," it signifies God's sovereign rule and reign, which is both a present spiritual reality and a future eschatological hope. It's not primarily a geographical territory but the dynamic exercise of divine authority.

Genitive plural of `οὐρανός`, meaning 'heaven' or 'sky'. In Matthew, "kingdom of heaven" is a circumlocution for "kingdom of God," used to avoid direct mention of God's name, reflecting Jewish reverence. It emphasizes the divine origin and transcendent nature of this kingdom.

3.meaning: Means 'a sound, tone, voice'. Here, it refers to the audible voice of John the Baptist, fulfilling the prophecy of Isaiah 40:3. It emphasizes the prophetic authority and divine origin of John's message, serving as God's herald in the wilderness.

Participle of `βοάω`, meaning 'to cry aloud, shout'. It describes the intense and urgent nature of John's proclamation. His voice was not a whisper but a powerful, public declaration, demanding attention and response in the desolate wilderness.

Aorist imperative of `ἑτοιμάζω`, meaning 'to prepare, make ready'. This command, drawn from Isaiah, calls for active preparation for the Lord's arrival, both practically (clearing paths) and spiritually (removing obstacles of sin and pride).

Genitive of `Κύριος`, meaning 'Lord, master'. In this context, referring to Yahweh in the Old Testament prophecy, it is applied to Jesus, identifying Him as the divine Lord for whom John prepares the way. It signifies His supreme authority and deity.

7.meaning: From Aramaic, meaning 'separated ones'. A prominent Jewish religious and political party known for strict adherence to the Law and oral traditions. John's rebuke highlights their hypocrisy and reliance on external observance over internal transformation.

A Jewish religious party, primarily aristocratic priests, who rejected oral tradition, resurrection, and angels. They were often politically aligned with Roman rule. John's condemnation shows that neither their legalism nor their skepticism exempted them from the need for repentance.

Plural of `γέννημα`, meaning 'offspring, brood'. Here, used metaphorically as "generation of vipers" (`ἐχιδνῶν`, G2191), it describes their corrupt nature and malicious intent, likening them to venomous snakes. It's a strong condemnation of their spiritual state.

Genitive of `ὀργή`, meaning 'wrath, indignation'. Refers to God's righteous and holy anger against sin. John warns them to flee from the "wrath to come," emphasizing the impending divine judgment for unrepentant sin.

11.meaning: Means 'to immerse, dip, baptize'. John's baptism was with water for repentance. The word implies a complete submersion, symbolizing cleansing and a new beginning. It is distinct from Christian baptism in its theological significance but shares the ritual act.

Comparative form of `ἰσχυρός`, meaning 'stronger, mightier'. John uses this to describe Jesus, acknowledging His superior power, authority, and divine nature. It highlights the vast difference in their respective ministries and persons.

Plural of `ὑπόδημα`, meaning 'sandals, shoes'. To carry or untie someone's sandals was a task for the lowest servant. John's statement emphasizes his profound humility and unworthiness in comparison to Jesus, underscoring Jesus' supreme dignity.

Dative of `Πνεῦμα`, meaning 'spirit, wind, breath'. Here, it refers to the Holy Spirit, the third person of the Trinity. Jesus' baptism with the Holy Spirit signifies the Spirit's empowering presence and indwelling in believers, initiating the new covenant.

Dative of `πῦρ`, meaning 'fire'. In the context of baptism, "fire" can symbolize purification and refining for believers, or destructive judgment for the unrepentant. It represents the intense, transformative, and often severe work of God.

12.meaning: A 'winnowing fork' or 'fan'. This agricultural tool was used to separate grain from chaff. It symbolizes Christ's role as judge, separating the righteous (wheat) from the wicked (chaff) in the final judgment.

Future indicative of `διακαθαρίζω`, meaning 'to thoroughly cleanse, purge'. It describes the thoroughness of Christ's judgment, where every impurity and unrighteousness will be completely removed from His "threshing floor" (the world or His people).

Means 'wheat, grain'. Symbolically represents the righteous, those who have genuinely repented and bear good fruit. They are gathered into God's "garner" (ἀποθήκην, G596), signifying salvation and eternal security.
Means 'chaff'. Symbolically represents the unrighteous, those who are spiritually barren and lack true repentance. They are light and worthless, destined for destruction.
Dative of `ἄσβεστος`, meaning 'unquenchable, inextinguishable'. Describes the eternal and unending nature of the fire of judgment, emphasizing the permanence of the consequences for the unrepentant.

15.meaning: Participle of `πρέπω`, meaning 'it is fitting, proper, becoming'. Jesus uses this to explain why His baptism is necessary, emphasizing that it aligns with divine propriety and God's established order for salvation.

Aorist infinitive of `πληρόω`, meaning 'to fulfill, complete, bring to an end'. Jesus' baptism was an act of fulfilling God's righteous requirements, not for His own sin, but as a representative act for humanity and to inaugurate His messianic mission.

Accusative of `δικαιοσύνη`, meaning 'righteousness, justice'. Here, "all righteousness" refers to God's righteous will and requirements. Jesus' baptism was an act of perfect obedience, demonstrating His commitment to God's plan for human redemption.

17.meaning: 'Voice, sound'. Here, it refers to the audible voice of God the Father from heaven, confirming Jesus' identity. This divine utterance provides authoritative testimony to Jesus' unique status.

'Son'. This title, "My beloved Son," declares Jesus' unique divine Sonship, emphasizing His intimate relationship with the Father and His divine nature. It echoes Old Testament messianic prophecies (Ps 2:7).
'Beloved, dear'. This adjective emphasizes the Father's deep affection and unique love for Jesus. It signifies Jesus' special status and favor with God, highlighting His preeminence and chosenness.

Aorist indicative of `εὐδοκέω`, meaning 'to be well pleased, to delight in'. This verb expresses the Father's complete satisfaction and approval of Jesus, both in His person and in His obedient act of baptism, marking the inauguration of His redemptive work.

meta.overview: Matthew 4 marks a pivotal transition in Jesus' ministry, detailing his temptation in the wilderness, the commencement of his public preaching, and the calling of his first disciples. Following his baptism, Jesus confronts Satan, demonstrating his perfect obedience and reliance on Scripture, thereby reversing Adam's fall. His subsequent relocation to Capernaum fulfills Isaiah's prophecy, signaling the dawn of God's kingdom in Galilee of the Gentiles. The chapter culminates with Jesus proclaiming the kingdom's imminence and gathering key followers, Simon Peter, Andrew, James, and John, who leave everything to become "fishers of men," illustrating the radical call to discipleship and the expansive reach of his healing and teaching ministry. This chapter establishes foundational themes of Christ's authority, the nature of temptation, the call to repentance, and the inauguration of the new covenant community.
meta.theme: Jesus' Temptation, Inauguration of Ministry, and Call to Discipleship
meta.keyWordMeaning: βασιλεία (basileia) refers to "kingdom" or "reign." Etymologically, it denotes the sphere of a king's rule or the act of ruling itself. Theologically, in Matthew, it signifies the sovereign rule of God, inaugurated by Jesus Christ, which is both a present reality (the "kingdom of heaven is at hand") and a future hope. It encompasses God's redemptive activity and the community that submits to His divine authority.
outline: Section 1 (vv.1-11): The Temptation of Jesus in the Wilderness
Section 2 (vv.12-16): Jesus Begins His Galilean Ministry and Fulfills Prophecy
Section 3 (vv.17-22): Jesus Proclaims the Kingdom and Calls First Disciples
Section 4 (vv.23-25): Jesus' Extensive Ministry of Teaching, Preaching, and Healing

verses.kjv_text: Then was Jesus led up of the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted of the devil.
verses.original_text: Τότε ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἀνήχθη εἰς τὴν ἔρημον ὑπὸ τοῦ Πνεύματος πειρασθῆναι ὑπὸ τοῦ διαβόλου.
verses.transliteration: Tote ho Iēsous anēchthē eis tēn erēmon hypo tou Pneumatos peirasthēnai hypo tou diabolou.
verses.study_note: This verse immediately follows Jesus' baptism, where he was affirmed as God's Son (Matt 3:17). The Spirit, which descended upon him, now "leads" (ἀνήχθη, 'anēchthē', 'was led up') him into the wilderness, not to fall, but to confront the devil. This event parallels Israel's forty years in the wilderness and foreshadows Jesus' role as the true Israel, obedient where they failed. The purpose is "to be tempted" (πειρασθῆναι, 'peirasthēnai'), highlighting a divine appointment for Christ to overcome evil at the outset of his public ministry.
verses.doctrinal_note: This passage demonstrates the active role of the Holy Spirit in Jesus' life and ministry, even in leading him into a situation of intense spiritual conflict. It underscores Christ's humanity, as he was truly subject to temptation, yet his divine nature ensured his sinless victory over the devil, setting the stage for his redemptive work.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had fasted forty days and forty nights, he was afterward an hungred.
verses.original_text: καὶ νηστεύσας ἡμέρας τεσσεράκοντα καὶ νύκτας τεσσεράκοντα ὕστερον ἐπείνασεν.
verses.transliteration: kai nēsteusas hēmeras tesserakonta kai nyktas tesserakonta hysteron epeinasen.
verses.study_note: Jesus' forty-day fast echoes Moses' fast on Mount Sinai (Exod 34:28) and Elijah's journey to Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8), both significant figures in Israel's history. This period of intense spiritual preparation and physical deprivation left him "hungry" (ἐπείνασεν, 'epeinasen'), making him vulnerable to the devil's specific temptations related to physical needs, power, and identity. This human weakness is crucial for understanding the reality of his temptation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' forty-day fast and subsequent hunger affirm his full humanity, experiencing physical limitations just as humans do. His endurance through this period, culminating in genuine hunger, underscores the reality of his temptation and the profound nature of his identification with humanity in its weakness, yet without sin.

verses.kjv_text: And when the tempter came to him, he said, If thou be the Son of God, command that these stones be made bread.
verses.original_text: καὶ προσελθὼν ὁ πειράζων εἶπεν αὐτῷ, Εἰ υἱὸς εἶ τοῦ Θεοῦ, εἰπὲ ἵνα οἱ λίθοι οὗτοι ἄρτοι γένωνται.
verses.transliteration: kai proselthōn ho peirazōn eipen autō, Ei huios ei tou Theou, eipe hina hoi lithoi houtoi artoi genōntai.
verses.study_note: The "tempter" (ὁ πειράζων, 'ho peirazōn'), a direct reference to Satan, challenges Jesus' identity as the "Son of God," echoing the divine declaration at his baptism (Matt 3:17). The temptation to turn stones into bread appeals to Jesus' physical hunger and the desire to use divine power for personal gain, bypassing the path of suffering and obedience. This tests his trust in God's provision and timing.
verses.doctrinal_note: Satan's opening challenge, "If you are the Son of God," directly attacks Jesus' divine identity and mission. This temptation to use divine power for self-gratification, rather than relying on God's provision, highlights the core of sin: self-will over divine will. Jesus' refusal demonstrates perfect obedience and trust in the Father.

verses.kjv_text: But he answered and said, It is written, Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν, Γέγραπται, Οὐκ ἐπ’ ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος, ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ παντὶ ῥήματι ἐκπορευομένῳ διὰ στόματος Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: ho de apokritheis eipen, Gegraptai, Ouk ep' artō monō zēsetai ho anthrōpos, all' epi panti rhēmati ekporeuomenō dia stomatos Theou.
verses.study_note: Jesus responds with Scripture, quoting Deuteronomy 8:3. This emphasizes the supremacy of God's word over physical needs and the importance of spiritual sustenance. His reliance on "every word that proceeds out of the mouth of God" demonstrates perfect faith and obedience, contrasting with Israel's grumbling for food in the wilderness. This sets a pattern for resisting temptation through biblical truth.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' immediate recourse to "It is written" establishes the absolute authority of Scripture as the ultimate defense against temptation and deception. His affirmation that "man shall not live by bread alone" underscores the spiritual nature of human existence and the necessity of God's revealed truth for true life and sustenance.

verses.kjv_text: Then the devil taketh him up into the holy city, and setteth him on a pinnacle of the temple,
verses.original_text: Τότε παραλαμβάνει αὐτὸν ὁ διάβολος εἰς τὴν ἁγίαν πόλιν, καὶ ἔστησεν αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὸ πτερύγιον τοῦ ἱεροῦ,
verses.transliteration: Tote paralambanei auton ho diabolos eis tēn hagian polin, kai estēsen auton epi to pterygion tou hierou.
verses.study_note: The devil takes Jesus to "the holy city," Jerusalem, and places him on the "pinnacle of the temple." This location, likely a high point overlooking the Kidron Valley, would have been a prominent and symbolic place. The shift from physical hunger to a temptation involving public display and spiritual pride indicates Satan's escalating strategy, aiming to exploit Jesus' messianic identity in a different way.

verses.kjv_text: And saith unto him, If thou be the Son of God, cast thyself down: for it is written, He shall give his angels charge concerning thee: and in their hands they shall bear thee up, lest at any time thou dash thy foot against a stone.
verses.original_text: καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ, Εἰ υἱὸς εἶ τοῦ Θεοῦ, βάλε σεαυτὸν κάτω· γέγραπται γάρ, ὅτι Τοῖς ἀγγέλοις αὐτοῦ ἐντελεῖται περὶ σοῦ, καὶ ἐπὶ χειρῶν ἀροῦσίν σε, μήποτε προσκόψῃς πρὸς λίθον τὸν πόδα σου.
verses.transliteration: kai legei autō, Ei huios ei tou Theou, bale seauton katō; gegraptai gar, hoti Tois angelois autou enteleitai peri sou, kai epi cheirōn arousīn se, mēpote proskopsēs pros lithon ton poda sou.
verses.study_note: Satan now quotes Scripture (Psalm 91:11-12), twisting its meaning to tempt Jesus to presumption and a spectacular display of power. The challenge is to force God's hand, demanding miraculous intervention to prove his Sonship and gain public acclaim, bypassing the humble path of suffering. This highlights the danger of misinterpreting or misapplying God's word.
verses.doctrinal_note: This temptation reveals Satan's cunning use of Scripture, twisting its meaning to promote presumption and test God. Jesus' refusal demonstrates that true faith does not demand signs or put God to the test, but trusts in His sovereign timing and method. It underscores the importance of interpreting Scripture in context and not using it for self-serving purposes.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus said unto him, It is written again, Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God.
verses.original_text: ἔφη αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Πάλιν γέγραπται, Οὐκ ἐκπειράσεις Κύριον τὸν Θεόν σου.
verses.transliteration: ephē autō ho Iēsous, Palin gegraptai, Ouk ekpeiraseis Kyrion ton Theon sou.
verses.study_note: Jesus again responds with Scripture, quoting Deuteronomy 6:16. He corrects Satan's misuse of Psalm 91 by appealing to a complementary truth: one must not "test the Lord your God." To jump from the temple would be to demand a miracle, forcing God's hand, rather than trusting in His providential care. This teaches the proper reverence and submission due to God.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' counter-quote, "You shall not put the Lord your God to the test," establishes a crucial principle: faith trusts God's promises without demanding proof or presumptuously placing oneself in danger. It teaches reverence for God's sovereignty and a rejection of any attempt to manipulate divine power for personal validation or spectacle.

verses.kjv_text: Again, the devil taketh him up into an exceeding high mountain, and sheweth him all the kingdoms of the world, and the glory of them;
verses.original_text: Πάλιν παραλαμβάνει αὐτὸν ὁ διάβολος εἰς ὄρος ὑψηλὸν λίαν, καὶ δείκνυσιν αὐτῷ πάσας τὰς βασιλείας τοῦ κόσμου καὶ τὴν δόξαν αὐτῶν,
verses.transliteration: Palin paralambanei auton ho diabolos eis oros hypsēlon lian, kai deiknysin autō pasas tas basileias tou kosmou kai tēn doxan autōn.
verses.study_note: The third temptation takes Jesus to an "exceeding high mountain," from which Satan shows him "all the kingdoms of the world and their glory." This is a vision of worldly power and dominion, appealing to the desire for universal kingship. It represents the ultimate shortcut to messianic rule, bypassing the cross and the path of suffering.

verses.kjv_text: And saith unto him, All these things will I give thee, if thou wilt fall down and worship me.
verses.original_text: καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ, Ταῦτά σοι πάντα δώσω, ἐὰν πεσὼν προσκυνήσῃς μοι.
verses.transliteration: kai legei autō, Tauta soi panta dōsō, ean pesōn proskynēsēs moi.
verses.study_note: Satan offers Jesus immediate universal dominion, but at the cost of worshiping him. This reveals the devil's true ambition: to usurp God's rightful place and receive worship. It's a direct challenge to Jesus' ultimate mission to establish God's kingdom and receive all worship. This temptation exposes the spiritual battle for ultimate allegiance.
verses.doctrinal_note: This temptation represents the ultimate spiritual battle: who will be worshipped? Satan's offer of worldly power in exchange for worship reveals his true nature as God's adversary, seeking to divert allegiance from the Creator. Jesus' refusal affirms God's exclusive right to worship and highlights the incompatibility of God's kingdom with worldly power gained through compromise with evil.

verses.kjv_text: Then saith Jesus unto him, Get thee hence, Satan: for it is written, Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve.
verses.original_text: Τότε λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς, Ὕπαγε, Σατανᾶ· γέγραπται γάρ, Κύριον τὸν Θεόν σου προσκυνήσεις, καὶ αὐτῷ μόνῳ λατρεύσεις.
verses.transliteration: Tote legei autō ho Iēsous, Hypage, Satana; gegraptai gar, Kyrion ton Theon sou proskynēseis, kai autō monō latreuseis.
verses.study_note: Jesus decisively rebukes Satan, commanding him to "Get thee hence" (Ὕπαγε, Σατανᾶ, 'Hypage, Satana'). He again quotes Scripture, Deuteronomy 6:13, emphasizing exclusive worship and service to God alone. This final victory over temptation establishes Jesus' authority over evil and his unwavering commitment to the Father's will, even unto death.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' definitive command, "Get thee hence, Satan," coupled with the Scripture "You shall worship the Lord your God, and Him only shall you serve," marks a decisive victory over evil. It establishes the absolute sovereignty of God and the exclusive nature of true worship, rejecting any compromise with the forces of darkness. This victory foreshadows Christ's ultimate triumph over Satan.

verses.kjv_text: Then the devil leaveth him, and, behold, angels came and ministered unto him.
verses.original_text: Τότε ἀφίησιν αὐτὸν ὁ διάβολος, καὶ ἰδοὺ ἄγγελοι προσῆλθον καὶ διηκόνουν αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: Tote aphīēsin auton ho diabolos, kai idou angeloi prosēlthon kai diēkonoun autō.
verses.study_note: Following Jesus' victory, the devil departs, and "angels came and ministered unto him." This angelic service provides physical and spiritual sustenance after his arduous ordeal, confirming God's approval and care. It echoes the angels ministering to Elijah (1 Kgs 19:5-8) and highlights the divine vindication of Jesus' obedience.

verses.kjv_text: Now when Jesus had heard that John was cast into prison, he departed into Galilee;
verses.original_text: Ἀκούσας δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι Ἰωάννης παρεδόθη, ἀνεχώρησεν εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν.
verses.transliteration: Akousas de ho Iēsous hoti Iōannēs paredothē, anechōrēsen eis tēn Galilaian.
verses.study_note: Jesus' departure into Galilee is prompted by the news of John the Baptist's arrest ("παρεδόθη," 'paredothē', 'was delivered up'). This event marks a turning point, signaling the end of John's preparatory ministry and the full commencement of Jesus' public ministry. It also suggests a strategic withdrawal from Judea, where John's arrest indicated growing opposition.

verses.kjv_text: And leaving Nazareth, he came and dwelt in Capernaum, which is upon the sea coast, in the borders of Zabulon and Nephthalim:
verses.original_text: καὶ καταλιπὼν τὴν Ναζαρὲτ ἐλθὼν κατῴκησεν εἰς Καφαρναοὺμ τὴν παραθαλασσίαν ἐν ὁρίοις Ζαβουλὼν καὶ Νεφθαλίμ,
verses.transliteration: kai katalipōn tēn Nazaret elthōn katōkēsen eis Kaphernaoum tēn parathalassian en horiois Zabulōn kai Nephthaleim.
verses.study_note: Jesus leaves Nazareth, his hometown, and settles in Capernaum, a bustling fishing town on the Sea of Galilee. This move is significant, placing his ministry in a more public and strategic location, specifically in the regions of Zebulun and Naphtali. This geographical detail is crucial for Matthew's theological argument in the following verses.

verses.kjv_text: That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet, saying,
verses.original_text: ἵνα πληρωθῇ τὸ ῥηθὲν διὰ Ἠσαΐου τοῦ προφήτου λέγοντος,
verses.transliteration: hina plērōthē to rhēthen dia Hēsaiou tou prophētou legontos,
verses.study_note: Matthew explicitly states that Jesus' relocation to Capernaum fulfills prophecy. This is a common Matthean motif, emphasizing Jesus' identity as the Messiah who perfectly embodies the Old Testament prophecies. The phrase "that it might be fulfilled" (ἵνα πληρωθῇ, 'hina plērōthē') highlights divine intentionality in Jesus' actions.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse underscores Matthew's consistent theme of Jesus as the fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy. It demonstrates God's sovereign plan unfolding precisely as foretold, validating Jesus' messianic claims and emphasizing the continuity between the Old Covenant promises and the New Covenant reality.

verses.kjv_text: The land of Zabulon, and the land of Nephthalim, by the way of the sea, beyond Jordan, Galilee of the Gentiles;
verses.original_text: Γῆ Ζαβουλὼν καὶ γῆ Νεφθαλίμ, ὁδὸν θαλάσσης, πέραν τοῦ Ἰορδάνου, Γαλιλαία τῶν ἐθνῶν,
verses.transliteration: Gē Zabulōn kai gē Nephthaleim, hodon thalassēs, peran tou Iordanou, Galilaia tōn ethnōn,
verses.study_note: This verse quotes Isaiah 9:1 (LXX), identifying the specific regions where Jesus settled. "Galilee of the Gentiles" (Γαλιλαία τῶν ἐθνῶν, 'Galilaia tōn ethnōn') refers to a region with a mixed Jewish and Gentile population, often considered religiously impure by Judeans. This setting foreshadows the universal scope of Jesus' mission, extending beyond Israel to the Gentiles (Matt 28:19).

verses.kjv_text: The people which sat in darkness saw great light; and to them which sat in the region and shadow of death light is sprung up.
verses.original_text: ὁ λαὸς ὁ καθήμενος ἐν σκοτίᾳ φῶς εἶδεν μέγα, καὶ τοῖς καθημένοις ἐν χώρᾳ καὶ σκιᾷ θανάτου φῶς ἀνέτειλεν αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: ho laos ho kathēmenos en skotia phōs eiden mega, kai tois kathēmenois en chōra kai skia thanatou phōs aneteilen autois.
verses.study_note: Continuing the quote from Isaiah 9:2, this verse describes the spiritual condition of the people in Galilee as "sitting in darkness" and "in the region and shadow of death." Jesus' arrival is depicted as the dawning of "great light," symbolizing salvation, truth, and hope. This highlights the transformative power of Christ's presence and message in a spiritually desolate region.
verses.doctrinal_note: This prophecy's fulfillment emphasizes Jesus as the "great light" who dispels spiritual darkness and the "shadow of death." It signifies the arrival of salvation and divine revelation to those previously without hope, particularly in a region considered marginalized. This establishes Christ as the source of life and truth for all humanity.

verses.kjv_text: From that time Jesus began to preach, and to say, Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.
verses.original_text: Ἀπὸ τότε ἤρξατο ὁ Ἰησοῦς κηρύσσειν καὶ λέγειν, Μετανοεῖτε· ἤγγικεν γὰρ ἡ βασιλεία τῶν οὐρανῶν.
verses.transliteration: Apo tote ērxato ho Iēsous kēryssein kai legein, Metanoeite; ēggiken gar hē basileia tōn ouranōn.
verses.study_note: This verse marks the official beginning of Jesus' public preaching ministry. His message, "Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand," echoes John the Baptist's message (Matt 3:2), but with the crucial difference that the King himself is now present. "Repent" (Μετανοεῖτε, 'Metanoeite') implies a change of mind and direction, while "kingdom of heaven" (ἡ βασιλεία τῶν οὐρανῶν, 'hē basileia tōn ouranōn') refers to God's active rule, now breaking into human history through Jesus.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' inaugural message, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand," is foundational. It calls for a radical change of heart and mind (metanoia) in response to the imminent arrival of God's sovereign rule through Christ. This kingdom is not merely future but present, demanding an immediate and transformative response of faith and obedience.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus, walking by the sea of Galilee, saw two brethren, Simon called Peter, and Andrew his brother, casting a net into the sea: for they were fishers.
verses.original_text: Περιπατῶν δὲ παρὰ τὴν θάλασσαν τῆς Γαλιλαίας εἶδεν δύο ἀδελφούς, Σίμωνα τὸν λεγόμενον Πέτρον καὶ Ἀνδρέαν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ, βάλλοντας ἀμφίβληστρον εἰς τὴν θάλασσαν· ἦσαν γὰρ ἁλιεῖς.
verses.transliteration: Peripatōn de para tēn thalassan tēs Galilaias eiden duo adelphous, Simōna ton legomenon Petron kai Andrean ton adelphon autou, ballontas amphiblēstron eis tēn thalassan; ēsan gar halieis.
verses.study_note: As Jesus walks by the Sea of Galilee, he encounters two brothers, Simon (later called Peter) and Andrew, who are fishermen. This setting highlights Jesus' ministry among ordinary people in their daily lives. The calling of these first disciples is a direct, personal invitation, initiating the formation of his inner circle and the new community of faith.

verses.kjv_text: And he saith unto them, Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.
verses.original_text: καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς, Δεῦτε ὀπίσω μου, καὶ ποιήσω ὑμᾶς ἁλιεῖς ἀνθρώπων.
verses.transliteration: kai legei autois, Deute opisō mou, kai poiēsō hymas halieis anthrōpōn.
verses.study_note: Jesus issues a direct call: "Follow me" (Δεῦτε ὀπίσω μου, 'Deute opisō mou'). This is an invitation to discipleship, demanding a radical reorientation of life. He promises to make them "fishers of men" (ἁλιεῖς ἀνθρώπων, 'halieis anthrōpōn'), transforming their earthly profession into a spiritual mission of gathering people into God's kingdom. This metaphor is powerful and easily understood by the fishermen.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' call to "Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men" is a foundational statement of discipleship. It signifies a radical reorientation of life, leaving behind former pursuits to embrace a new, kingdom-focused mission. The transformation from fishing for fish to fishing for people illustrates the spiritual purpose of the church: to gather souls for Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And they straightway left their nets, and followed him.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ εὐθέως ἀφέντες τὰ δίκτυα ἠκολούθησαν αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: hoi de eutheōs aphentes ta diktya ēkolouthēsan autō.
verses.study_note: The immediate and decisive response of Simon and Andrew—"straightway left their nets, and followed him"—demonstrates profound faith and obedience. Their willingness to abandon their livelihood and familiar life for an unknown future with Jesus exemplifies the radical commitment required for true discipleship. This immediate response sets a precedent for future calls.

verses.kjv_text: And going on from thence, he saw other two brethren, James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother, in a ship with Zebedee their father, mending their nets; and he called them.
verses.original_text: Καὶ προβὰς ἐκεῖθεν εἶδεν ἄλλους δύο ἀδελφούς, Ἰάκωβον τὸν τοῦ Ζεβεδαίου καὶ Ἰωάννην τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ, ἐν τῷ πλοίῳ μετὰ Ζεβεδαίου τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτῶν καταρτίζοντας τὰ δίκτυα αὐτῶν, καὶ ἐκάλεσεν αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: Kai probas ekeithen eiden allous duo adelphous, Iakōbon ton tou Zebedaiou kai Iōannēn ton adelphon autou, en tō ploiō meta Zebedaiou tou patros autōn katartizontas ta diktya autōn, kai ekalesen autous.
verses.study_note: Jesus continues along the coast and calls two more brothers, James and John, sons of Zebedee. They are also engaged in their fishing trade, "mending their nets." This detail suggests diligence and preparation, qualities Jesus would harness for their future ministry. The calling of these four forms the core of his initial discipleship group.

verses.kjv_text: And they immediately left the ship and their father, and followed him.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ εὐθέως ἀφέντες τὸ πλοῖον καὶ τὸν πατέρα αὐτῶν ἠκολούθησαν αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: hoi de eutheōs aphentes to ploion kai ton patera autōn ēkolouthēsan autō.
verses.study_note: Like Simon and Andrew, James and John respond "immediately," leaving not only their boat but also their father. This act signifies an even greater sacrifice, severing familial ties and economic security for the sake of following Jesus. Their radical obedience highlights the supreme priority of Christ's call over all earthly allegiances.
verses.doctrinal_note: The immediate and radical obedience of James and John, leaving their boat and even their father, exemplifies the cost of discipleship. It underscores the supreme priority of Christ's call, demanding a willingness to forsake all earthly ties and comforts for the sake of the kingdom. This sets a high standard for commitment.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus went about all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing all manner of sickness and all manner of disease among the people.
verses.original_text: Καὶ περιῆγεν ἐν ὅλῃ τῇ Γαλιλαίᾳ, διδάσκων ἐν ταῖς συναγωγαῖς αὐτῶν καὶ κηρύσσων τὸ εὐαγγέλιον τῆς βασιλείας καὶ θεραπεύων πᾶσαν νόσον καὶ πᾶσαν μαλακίαν ἐν τῷ λαῷ.
verses.transliteration: Kai periēgen en holē tē Galilaia, didaskōn en tais synagōgais autōn kai kēryssōn to euangelion tēs basileias kai therapeuōn pasan noson kai pasan malakian en tō laō.
verses.study_note: This verse provides a summary of Jesus' comprehensive ministry throughout Galilee. It highlights three key aspects: "teaching in their synagogues" (instruction), "preaching the gospel of the kingdom" (proclamation), and "healing all manner of sickness and all manner of disease" (demonstration of power). These activities collectively manifest the arrival of God's kingdom.
verses.doctrinal_note: This summary verse encapsulates the multifaceted nature of Jesus' ministry: teaching (discipleship), preaching the gospel of the kingdom (evangelism), and healing (compassion and demonstration of divine power). These three pillars reveal Christ's holistic approach to human need, addressing spiritual, intellectual, and physical brokenness as manifestations of the kingdom's arrival.

verses.kjv_text: And his fame went throughout all Syria: and they brought unto him all sick people that were taken with divers diseases and torments, and those which were possessed with devils, and those which were lunatick, and those that had the palsy; and he healed them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπῆλθεν ἡ ἀκοὴ αὐτοῦ εἰς ὅλην τὴν Συρίαν· καὶ προσήνεγκαν αὐτῷ πάντας τοὺς κακῶς ἔχοντας ποικίλαις νόσοις καὶ βασάνοις συνεχομένους, δαιμονιζομένους καὶ σεληνιαζομένους καὶ παραλυτικούς, καὶ ἐθεράπευσεν αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: kai apēlthen hē akoē autou eis holēn tēn Syrian; kai prosēnenkan autō pantas tous kakōs echontas poikilais nosois kai basanois synechomenous, daimonizomenous kai selēniazomenous kai paralytikous, kai etherapeusen autous.
verses.study_note: Jesus' fame spreads rapidly beyond Galilee, reaching "all Syria." People bring him a wide array of sufferers: those with "divers diseases and torments," "possessed with devils," "lunatick" (epileptic), and "palsy" (paralytics). The comprehensive list emphasizes the extent of human suffering and Jesus' universal power to heal all conditions, demonstrating his divine authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' widespread healing ministry, encompassing diverse ailments and demonic possession, powerfully demonstrates his divine authority over sickness, sin, and evil. These miracles are not mere acts of compassion but tangible signs of the kingdom of God breaking into the world, confirming Jesus' identity as the Messiah and the power of the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: And there followed him great multitudes of people from Galilee, and from Decapolis, and from Jerusalem, and from Judaea, and from beyond Jordan.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἠκολούθησαν αὐτῷ ὄχλοι πολλοὶ ἀπὸ τῆς Γαλιλαίας καὶ Δεκαπόλεως καὶ Ἱεροσολύμων καὶ Ἰουδαίας καὶ πέραν τοῦ Ἰορδάνου.
verses.transliteration: kai ēkolouthēsan autō ochloi polloi apo tēs Galilaias kai Dekapoleōs kai Hierosolymōn kai Ioudaias kai peran tou Iordanou.
verses.study_note: The chapter concludes with "great multitudes" following Jesus from various regions: Galilee, Decapolis (a region of ten Hellenistic cities), Jerusalem, Judea, and Transjordan. This geographical scope highlights the immense impact of Jesus' ministry and the widespread interest he generated, drawing people from diverse backgrounds and locations to witness his teaching and miracles.

1.meaning: From `anagō` (to lead up, bring up). Here, passive, "was led up." Theologically, it implies divine agency; Jesus was not merely wandering but purposefully directed by the Spirit. This highlights God's sovereign orchestration of events, even in leading Christ into temptation for a greater redemptive purpose.

From `pneuma` (spirit, wind, breath). Refers to the Holy Spirit. In this context, the Spirit's active role in guiding Jesus underscores the Trinitarian nature of God's work and the Spirit's empowerment for ministry and confrontation with evil.

From `peirazō` (to test, tempt, try). Here, a passive infinitive, "to be tempted/tested." This word carries the nuance of both testing for proof and tempting to sin. Jesus' temptation was a divine test to prove his obedience and sinlessness, not an enticement to fall into sin.

From `diabolos` (slanderer, accuser). Refers to the devil, Satan. Etymologically, it means "to throw across" or "to slander." Theologically, it identifies the primary adversary of God and humanity, whose aim is to deceive, accuse, and lead astray from God's will.

3.meaning: Present active participle of `peirazō` (to test, tempt). Here, "the tempter," a specific title for Satan. This emphasizes his active and ongoing role in testing and trying to lead people away from God, particularly Jesus in this pivotal moment.

Son. Theologically, "Son of God" is a messianic and divine title. Satan's challenge, "If you are the Son of God," directly questions Jesus' identity and authority, aiming to provoke him to prove it outside of God's appointed way.
Stones. The temptation to turn stones into bread appeals to Jesus' physical hunger and the desire to use divine power for personal gratification, bypassing reliance on God's provision and timing.

Loaves of bread. Represents basic sustenance. The temptation here is to satisfy immediate physical needs through supernatural means, rather than trusting God's timing and method for provision, which would involve suffering.

4.meaning: Perfect passive indicative of `graphō` (to write). "It is written." This phrase, used repeatedly by Jesus, signifies the absolute authority and binding nature of Scripture. It is a declaration that God's word is settled and unchangeable, serving as the ultimate defense against temptation.

Bread. Here, in the dative, "by bread." Jesus' quote from Deuteronomy 8:3 asserts that physical sustenance is secondary to spiritual nourishment derived from God's word. It redefines true life as dependent on divine revelation, not merely material provision.

From `rhēma` (word, utterance, saying). Refers to a spoken word or divine utterance. In this context, "every word that proceeds out of the mouth of God" emphasizes the comprehensive and life-giving nature of God's entire revelation, which sustains humanity spiritually.
God. The ultimate source of all life and truth. Jesus' reliance on the "word of God" underscores the divine origin and authority of Scripture, making it the supreme guide for human conduct and faith.

10.meaning: Imperative of `hypagō` (go away, depart). A forceful command to depart. Jesus' direct and authoritative expulsion of Satan demonstrates his power over evil and his unwavering commitment to God's will, refusing any compromise with the adversary.

From Hebrew `satan` (adversary). The personal name of the devil. Jesus' direct address to "Satan" confirms his identity as the ultimate enemy and highlights the personal nature of the spiritual battle.

Future indicative of `proskyneō` (to worship, bow down). "You shall worship." This word denotes an act of profound reverence and submission, often involving prostration. Jesus' quote from Deuteronomy 6:13 asserts that worship is due exclusively to God, rejecting any form of idolatry or allegiance to evil.

Future indicative of `latreuō` (to serve, minister, worship). "You shall serve." This term implies devoted service, often in a religious context. Paired with `proskyneō`, it emphasizes that not only worship but also dedicated service and allegiance belong solely to God, rejecting any dual loyalty.

17.meaning: Aorist middle indicative of `archō` (to begin, rule). "He began." This marks a significant turning point, the official commencement of Jesus' public ministry after his baptism and temptation. It signals the transition from preparation to active proclamation of the kingdom.

Present active infinitive of `kēryssō` (to preach, proclaim as a herald). Jesus' primary activity was to publicly announce the good news. This term emphasizes authoritative, public proclamation, not merely teaching, but declaring a message of divine significance.

Present active imperative of `metanoeō` (to repent, change one's mind). "Repent!" This is a call for a fundamental change of heart, mind, and direction, turning away from sin and towards God. It is the initial and essential response required for entering the kingdom.

Kingdom, reign, royal power. "Kingdom of heaven" (a Matthean idiom for "kingdom of God"). It refers to God's sovereign rule, which is both a present reality inaugurated by Jesus and a future consummation. Its "at hand" (ἤγγικεν) signifies its imminent and active presence.

19.meaning: Imperative, "Come here! Come!" Often used as an invitation to follow. Jesus' direct and personal invitation to "Follow me" is a call to discipleship, demanding a radical commitment and reorientation of life around his person and mission.
Behind, after. "Follow me" (literally, "come after me"). This implies not just physical proximity but also adherence to Jesus' teachings, lifestyle, and mission, becoming his apprentice and imitator.

Future active indicative of `poieō` (to make, do, create). "I will make." This highlights Jesus' transformative power. He doesn't just invite them to a task but promises to equip and transform them for it, enabling them to fulfill their new calling.

Fishermen. The metaphor "fishers of men" (ἁλιεῖς ἀνθρώπων) transforms their familiar profession into a spiritual mission. Just as they caught fish for sustenance, they would now gather people into the kingdom of God for eternal life, emphasizing the urgency and purpose of evangelism.

23.meaning: Imperfect active indicative of `periagō` (to go about, travel around). "He went about." This describes Jesus' itinerant ministry, actively moving throughout Galilee to reach many people. It conveys the dynamic and expansive nature of his early ministry.

Present active participle of `didaskō` (to teach). "Teaching." This refers to systematic instruction, often in synagogues, explaining the meaning of Scripture and the nature of the kingdom. It highlights the intellectual and revelatory aspect of Jesus' ministry.

Present active participle of `kēryssō` (to preach, proclaim). "Preaching." This refers to the public proclamation of the good news (gospel) of the kingdom. It emphasizes the authoritative declaration of God's message, calling for a response.

Good news, gospel. "The gospel of the kingdom." This refers to the joyous message that God's saving rule has arrived in Jesus Christ, bringing forgiveness, healing, and new life. It is the central message of Jesus' ministry.

Present active participle of `therapeuō` (to heal, cure, serve). "Healing." This aspect of Jesus' ministry demonstrates his divine power over sickness and disease, serving as tangible proof of the kingdom's presence and his authority as Messiah.

meta.overview: Matthew Chapter 5 inaugurates the Sermon on the Mount, presenting Jesus' radical reinterpretation of the Mosaic Law and the nature of true righteousness. It begins with the Beatitudes, outlining the character traits of citizens in the Kingdom of Heaven, followed by their role as 'salt and light' in the world. Jesus then clarifies that he came not to abolish but to fulfill the Law and the Prophets, deepening their ethical demands from external actions to internal thoughts and intentions. He provides six antitheses, contrasting traditional interpretations ('You have heard that it was said...') with his authoritative teaching ('But I say to you...'), covering anger, lust, divorce, oaths, retaliation, and love for enemies. The chapter culminates in a call to perfection, mirroring God's own character, thereby establishing a foundational ethical framework for Christian discipleship that transcends mere legalism and calls for profound internal transformation.
meta.theme: The Kingdom Citizen's Radical Righteousness and Ethical Demands
meta.keyWordMeaning: From 'δίκαιος (dikaios)' meaning 'just, righteous'. In a biblical context, it refers to conformity to God's standard, both in character and action. It encompasses ethical uprightness, justice, and the state of being right with God. Jesus redefines it as an internal disposition exceeding mere external legalism, essential for kingdom entry and reflecting God's character.
outline: Section 1 (vv.1-12): The Character of Kingdom Citizens (The Beatitudes)
Section 2 (vv.13-16): The Influence of Kingdom Citizens (Salt and Light)
Section 3 (vv.17-20): Jesus and the Law: Fulfillment, Not Abolition
Section 4 (vv.21-26): Righteousness and Anger: Beyond Murder
Section 5 (vv.27-32): Righteousness and Lust: Beyond Adultery
Section 6 (vv.33-37): Righteousness and Oaths: Beyond False Swearing
Section 7 (vv.38-42): Righteousness and Retaliation: Beyond 'Eye for an Eye'
Section 8 (vv.43-48): Righteousness and Love: Beyond Loving Neighbors Only

verses.kjv_text: And seeing the multitudes, he went up into a mountain: and when he was set, his disciples came unto him:
verses.original_text: Ἰδὼν δὲ τοὺς ὄχλους, ἀνέβη εἰς τὸ ὄρος· καὶ καθίσαντος αὐτοῦ προσῆλθον αὐτῷ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ·
verses.transliteration: Idōn de tous ochlous, anebē eis to oros; kai kathisantos autou prosēlthon autō hoi mathētai autou:
verses.study_note: Jesus' ascent 'into a mountain' (τὸ ὄρος) recalls Moses receiving the Law on Mount Sinai, subtly positioning Jesus as a new Moses, delivering a new covenant teaching. The setting is intimate, with 'his disciples' (οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ) coming to him, though the 'multitudes' (τοὺς ὄχλους) are still in view, suggesting the teaching is for both inner circle and broader audience. This sets the stage for the Sermon on the Mount, a foundational discourse on kingdom ethics (Matt 5:2).

verses.kjv_text: And he opened his mouth, and taught them, saying,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀνοίξας τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ ἐδίδασκεν αὐτοὺς λέγων·
verses.transliteration: kai anoixas to stoma autou edidasken autous legōn:
verses.study_note: The phrase 'he opened his mouth' (ἀνοίξας τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ) is a Hebraism indicating a solemn and significant discourse is about to begin, emphasizing the importance of the words that follow. Jesus 'taught them' (ἐδίδασκεν αὐτοὺς), signifying his authority as a teacher (rabbi), distinct from the scribes. This verse transitions directly into the Beatitudes, the core of his ethical instruction (Matt 7:28-29).

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ πτωχοὶ τῷ πνεύματι, ὅτι αὐτῶν ἐστιν ἡ βασιλεία τῶν οὐρανῶν.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi ptōchoi tō pneumati, hoti autōn estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn.
verses.study_note: The first Beatitude declares 'blessed' (μακάριοι) those who are 'poor in spirit' (οἱ πτωχοὶ τῷ πνεύματι). This refers not to material poverty, but to a spiritual humility and dependence on God, acknowledging one's utter spiritual bankruptcy and need for divine grace. Such a disposition is foundational for receiving 'the kingdom of heaven' (ἡ βασιλεία τῶν οὐρανῶν), which is both a present reality and a future hope. This contrasts sharply with self-righteousness (Luke 18:9-14).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse introduces the core concept of spiritual humility as the gateway to God's kingdom. It teaches that true blessedness comes not from self-sufficiency or worldly status, but from recognizing one's spiritual need and dependence on God, a foundational principle for Christian discipleship and salvation.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ πενθοῦντες, ὅτι αὐτοὶ παρακληθήσονται.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi penthountes, hoti autoi paraklēthēsontai.
verses.study_note: The 'mourners' (οἱ πενθοῦντες) are those who grieve over sin—their own and the world's brokenness—and perhaps also those who suffer affliction for righteousness' sake. This mourning is not despair but a godly sorrow that leads to repentance (2 Cor 7:10). Their blessedness lies in the promise that 'they shall be comforted' (παρακληθήσονται), a comfort that comes from God's presence, forgiveness, and the hope of ultimate restoration.
verses.doctrinal_note: This Beatitude highlights the blessedness of godly sorrow, which recognizes the gravity of sin and the brokenness of the world. It promises divine comfort and restoration to those who genuinely mourn, pointing to the redemptive work of Christ and the ultimate hope of a new heaven and new earth.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ πραεῖς, ὅτι αὐτοὶ κληρονομήσουσιν τὴν γῆν.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi praeis, hoti autoi klēronomēsousin tēn gēn.
verses.study_note: The 'meek' (οἱ πραεῖς) are not weak, but gentle, humble, and submissive to God's will, exercising strength under control. This virtue is exemplified by Moses (Num 12:3) and Jesus himself (Matt 11:29). The promise to 'inherit the earth' (κληρονομήσουσιν τὴν γῆν) echoes Psalm 37:11, referring to a spiritual inheritance in God's kingdom, which includes both present blessings and future dominion in the new creation (Rev 5:10).
verses.doctrinal_note: Meekness, as taught here, is a Christ-like virtue of gentle strength and submission to God. It promises an inheritance of the earth, signifying not worldly power but participation in God's redemptive plan and a share in the renewed creation, demonstrating that true authority comes through humility.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness: for they shall be filled.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ πεινῶντες καὶ διψῶντες τὴν δικαιοσύνην, ὅτι αὐτοὶ χορτασθήσονται.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi peinōntes kai dipsōntes tēn dikaiosynēn, hoti autoi chortasthēsontai.
verses.study_note: This Beatitude describes an intense spiritual longing: 'hunger and thirst after righteousness' (πεινῶντες καὶ διψῶντες τὴν δικαιοσύνην). This is not merely a desire for justice, but for personal conformity to God's holy character and will. It implies a deep dissatisfaction with one's own spiritual state and a fervent pursuit of God's ways. The promise is that 'they shall be filled' (χορτασθήσονται), indicating complete satisfaction and spiritual fulfillment through Christ (John 6:35).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse underscores the blessedness of a profound spiritual yearning for God's righteousness. It teaches that those who earnestly seek to live in accordance with God's holy standards will find complete satisfaction and fulfillment in Him, highlighting the transformative power of divine grace.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain mercy.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ ἐλεήμονες, ὅτι αὐτοὶ ἐλεηθήσονται.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi eleēmones, hoti autoi eleēthēsontai.
verses.study_note: The 'merciful' (οἱ ἐλεήμονες) are those who show compassion and active kindness to others, especially those in need or distress. This mercy is not just an emotion but an action, reflecting God's own character (Luke 6:36). The reciprocal promise, 'they shall obtain mercy' (ἐλεηθήσονται), emphasizes that God's grace and forgiveness are extended to those who extend it to others, a principle reiterated in the Lord's Prayer (Matt 6:14-15).
verses.doctrinal_note: This Beatitude establishes mercy as a core attribute of kingdom citizens, reflecting God's character. It teaches a principle of divine reciprocity: those who show active compassion and kindness to others will themselves receive God's mercy, underscoring the importance of practical love in Christian living.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ καθαροὶ τῇ καρδίᾳ, ὅτι αὐτοὶ τὸν Θεὸν ὄψονται.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi katharoi tē kardia, hoti autoi ton Theon opsontai.
verses.study_note: To be 'pure in heart' (οἱ καθαροὶ τῇ καρδίᾳ) means to have an undivided devotion to God, free from hypocrisy, duplicity, and hidden sin. It refers to inner integrity and sincerity of motive, rather than mere outward conformity. The profound promise, 'they shall see God' (τὸν Θεὸν ὄψονται), speaks of an intimate, unhindered fellowship with God, both in this life through spiritual discernment and ultimately in the eschatological vision of His presence (1 John 3:2; Rev 22:4).
verses.doctrinal_note: Purity of heart is presented as essential for true spiritual vision and intimacy with God. This Beatitude teaches that genuine, internal integrity and undivided devotion to God enable believers to 'see' and experience Him, both spiritually in the present and ultimately in His glorious presence.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ εἰρηνοποιοί, ὅτι αὐτοὶ υἱοὶ Θεοῦ κληθήσονται.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi eirēnopoiē, hoti autoi huioi Theou klēthēsontai.
verses.study_note: The 'peacemakers' (οἱ εἰρηνοποιοί) are not merely passive lovers of peace, but active agents who work to reconcile conflicting parties and promote harmony. They embody God's own work of reconciliation through Christ (2 Cor 5:18-20). Their blessedness is that 'they shall be called the children of God' (υἱοὶ Θεοῦ κληθήσονται), reflecting their participation in God's character and mission to bring peace to a broken world (Rom 12:18).
verses.doctrinal_note: This Beatitude elevates active peacemaking to a divine characteristic, identifying those who work for reconciliation as 'children of God.' It underscores the Christian's calling to embody God's redemptive work in a fractured world, reflecting His nature as the ultimate Peacemaker.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness’ sake: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοι οἱ δεδιωγμένοι ἕνεκεν δικαιοσύνης, ὅτι αὐτῶν ἐστιν ἡ βασιλεία τῶν οὐρανῶν.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi hoi dediōgmenoi heneken dikaiosynēs, hoti autōn estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn.
verses.study_note: This Beatitude addresses the reality of suffering for faith: 'persecuted for righteousness' sake' (δεδιωγμένοι ἕνεκεν δικαιοσύνης). This persecution is a consequence of living out the kingdom ethics described in the preceding Beatitudes. It confirms that true discipleship often involves opposition from the world. The reward is the same as the first Beatitude: 'theirs is the kingdom of heaven' (αὐτῶν ἐστιν ἡ βασιλεία τῶν οὐρανῶν), emphasizing that suffering for Christ is a mark of genuine belonging to His kingdom (2 Tim 3:12).
verses.doctrinal_note: This Beatitude affirms that suffering for righteousness is a mark of true discipleship and a pathway to the Kingdom of Heaven. It teaches that persecution, when endured for Christ's sake, does not diminish but rather confirms one's blessed status and inheritance in God's eternal reign.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed are ye, when men shall revile you, and persecute you, and shall say all manner of evil against you falsely, for my sake.
verses.original_text: Μακάριοί ἐστε ὅταν ὀνειδίσωσιν ὑμᾶς καὶ διώξωσιν καὶ εἴπωσιν πᾶν πονηρὸν καθ’ ὑμῶν ψευδόμενοι ἕνεκεν ἐμοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Makarioi este hotan oneidisōsin humas kai diōxōsin kai eipōsin pan ponēron kath' humōn pseudomenoi heneken emou.
verses.study_note: This verse particularizes the previous Beatitude, shifting from 'they' to 'you,' making the application direct to the disciples. It specifies various forms of persecution: 'revile you, and persecute you, and shall say all manner of evil against you falsely' (ὀνειδίσωσιν ὑμᾶς καὶ διώξωσιν καὶ εἴπωσιν πᾶν πονηρὸν καθ’ ὑμῶν ψευδόμενοι). The crucial qualifier is 'for my sake' (ἕνεκεν ἐμοῦ), indicating that suffering for Christ is the true measure of blessedness, aligning believers with His own experience of rejection (John 15:20).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse clarifies that blessedness in persecution is specifically tied to suffering 'for my sake,' meaning for Christ and His gospel. It teaches that enduring false accusations and hostility as a result of one's allegiance to Jesus is a sign of genuine discipleship and a pathway to divine reward.

verses.kjv_text: Rejoice, and be exceeding glad: for great is your reward in heaven: for so persecuted they the prophets which were before you.
verses.original_text: χαίρετε καὶ ἀγαλλιᾶσθε, ὅτι ὁ μισθὸς ὑμῶν πολὺς ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς· οὕτως γὰρ ἐδίωξαν τοὺς προφήτας τοὺς πρὸ ὑμῶν.
verses.transliteration: chairete kai agalliasthe, hoti ho misthos humōn polys en tois ouranois; houtōs gar ediōxan tous prophētas tous pro humōn.
verses.study_note: Instead of despair, Jesus calls for 'rejoice, and be exceeding glad' (χαίρετε καὶ ἀγαλλιᾶσθε) in the face of persecution. The reason is the 'great reward in heaven' (ὁ μισθὸς ὑμῶν πολὺς ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς), emphasizing an eternal perspective. Furthermore, disciples are placed in the esteemed company of 'the prophets which were before you' (τοὺς προφήτας τοὺς πρὸ ὑμῶν), who also suffered for their faithfulness, providing a historical and theological precedent for Christian suffering (Acts 7:52).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse provides a powerful motivation for enduring persecution: a 'great reward in heaven' and solidarity with the persecuted prophets of old. It teaches believers to find joy in suffering for Christ, knowing their faithfulness will be eternally honored and that they walk in the footsteps of God's faithful witnesses.

verses.kjv_text: Ye are the salt of the earth: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be salted? it is thenceforth good for nothing, but to be cast out, and to be trodden under foot of men.
verses.original_text: Ὑμεῖς ἐστε τὸ ἅλας τῆς γῆς· ἐὰν δὲ τὸ ἅλας μωρανθῇ, ἐν τίνι ἁλισθήσεται; εἰς οὐδὲν ἰσχύει ἔτι εἰ μὴ βληθὲν ἔξω καταπατεῖσθαι ὑπὸ τῶν ἀνθρώπων.
verses.transliteration: Humeis este to halas tēs gēs; ean de to halas mōranthē, en tini halisthēsetai? eis ouden ischuei eti ei mē blēthen exō katapateisthai hypo tōn anthrōpōn.
verses.study_note: Jesus declares his disciples 'the salt of the earth' (τὸ ἅλας τῆς γῆς). Salt in the ancient world had two primary functions: preservation and flavor. Disciples are called to preserve society from moral decay and to add spiritual 'flavor' to the world. However, if 'the salt have lost his savour' (μωρανθῇ), meaning it becomes tasteless or ineffective, it is 'good for nothing' (εἰς οὐδὲν ἰσχύει ἔτι), emphasizing the critical importance of maintaining spiritual distinctiveness and influence (Col 4:6).
verses.doctrinal_note: This metaphor highlights the Christian's vital role in the world: to preserve moral integrity and enhance spiritual truth. It warns against spiritual compromise, emphasizing that a loss of distinctiveness renders believers ineffective and unable to fulfill their God-given purpose, with serious implications for their witness.

verses.kjv_text: Ye are the light of the world. A city that is set on an hill cannot be hid.
verses.original_text: Ὑμεῖς ἐστε τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου. οὐ δύναται πόλις κρυβῆναι ἐπάνω ὄρους κειμένη·
verses.transliteration: Humeis este to phōs tou kosmou. ou dynatai polis krybēnai epanō orous keimenē:
verses.study_note: Following the 'salt' metaphor, Jesus calls his disciples 'the light of the world' (τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου). Light illuminates, guides, and exposes. Believers are to reflect Christ, the true Light (John 8:12), into a world darkened by sin. The image of 'A city that is set on an hill cannot be hid' (πόλις κρυβῆναι ἐπάνω ὄρους κειμένη) emphasizes the public and visible nature of Christian witness. Disciples are meant to be seen and to shine brightly (Phil 2:15).
verses.doctrinal_note: This metaphor defines the Christian's public identity and mission: to illuminate the world with God's truth and righteousness. It teaches that believers are called to be visible examples of Christ's light, demonstrating God's presence and character in a way that cannot be concealed.

verses.kjv_text: Neither do men light a candle, and put it under a bushel, but on a candlestick; and it giveth light unto all that are in the house.
verses.original_text: οὐδὲ καίουσιν λύχνον καὶ τιθέασιν αὐτὸν ὑπὸ τὸν μόδιον ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ τὴν λυχνίαν, καὶ λάμπει πᾶσιν τοῖς ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ.
verses.transliteration: oude kaiousin lychnon kai titheasin auton hypo ton modion all' epi tēn lychnian, kai lampeipasins tois en tē oikia.
verses.study_note: This verse extends the 'light' metaphor with a practical illustration. No one lights a 'candle' (λύχνον) only to hide it 'under a bushel' (ὑπὸ τὸν μόδιον, a grain-measuring basket), but rather places it 'on a candlestick' (ἐπὶ τὴν λυχνίαν) so it can illuminate 'all that are in the house' (πᾶσιν τοῖς ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ). This reinforces the idea that Christian witness is meant to be visible and impactful, not concealed or privatized (Mark 4:21; Luke 8:16).
verses.doctrinal_note: This illustration emphasizes the imperative for believers to openly display their faith and good works. It teaches that Christian influence is meant to be public and pervasive, not hidden, so that it can effectively illuminate and benefit all within its sphere of reach.

verses.kjv_text: Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.
verses.original_text: Οὕτως λαμψάτω τὸ φῶς ὑμῶν ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀνθρώπων, ὅπως ἴδωσιν ὑμῶν τὰ καλὰ ἔργα καὶ δοξάσωσιν τὸν Πατέρα ὑμῶν τὸν ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς.
verses.transliteration: Houtōs lampsatō to phōs humōn emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn, hopōs idōsin humōn ta kala erga kai doxasōsin ton Paterahumon ton en tois ouranois.
verses.study_note: The purpose of shining 'your light' (τὸ φῶς ὑμῶν) 'before men' (ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀνθρώπων) is not self-glorification, but that others 'may see your good works' (ἴδωσιν ὑμῶν τὰ καλὰ ἔργα) and, crucially, 'glorify your Father which is in heaven' (δοξάσωσιν τὸν Πατέρα ὑμῶν τὸν ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς). This highlights the evangelistic and doxological aim of Christian living: our righteous actions should point others to God, not to ourselves (1 Pet 2:12).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse articulates the ultimate purpose of Christian witness: that believers' 'good works' should lead others to glorify God. It teaches that our transformed lives are meant to be a testimony to God's power and goodness, drawing people to acknowledge and worship our Heavenly Father.

verses.kjv_text: Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil.
verses.original_text: Μὴ νομίσητε ὅτι ἦλθον καταλῦσαι τὸν νόμον ἢ τοὺς προφήτας· οὐκ ἦλθον καταλῦσαι ἀλλὰ πληρῶσαι.
verses.transliteration: Mē nomisēte hoti ēlthen katalysai ton nomon ē tous prophētas; ouk ēlthen katalysai alla plērōsai.
verses.study_note: Jesus directly addresses a potential misunderstanding: 'Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets' (Μὴ νομίσητε ὅτι ἦλθον καταλῦσαι τὸν νόμον ἢ τοὺς προφήτας). 'The Law and the Prophets' refers to the entire Old Testament revelation. Jesus emphatically states, 'I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil' (οὐκ ἦλθον καταλῦσαι ἀλλὰ πληρῶσαι). 'To fulfil' (πληρῶσαι) means to bring to its intended purpose, to complete, to embody, and to perfectly obey, revealing its full spiritual meaning (Rom 10:4).
verses.doctrinal_note: This pivotal statement clarifies Jesus' relationship to the Old Testament. He did not abolish the Law but fulfilled it, both by perfectly obeying its demands and by revealing its ultimate spiritual intent. This teaches that Christ is the culmination of God's redemptive plan, bringing the Old Covenant to its intended purpose.

verses.kjv_text: For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled.
verses.original_text: Ἀμὴν γὰρ λέγω ὑμῖν, ἕως ἂν παρέλθῃ ὁ οὐρανὸς καὶ ἡ γῆ, ἰῶτα ἓν ἢ μία κεραία οὐ μὴ παρέλθῃ ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου, ἕως ἂν πάντα γένηται.
verses.transliteration: Amēn gar legō humin, heōs an parelthē ho ouranos kai hē gē, iōta hen ē mia keraia ou mē parelthē apo tou nomou, heōs an panta genētai.
verses.study_note: Jesus uses a strong affirmation, 'Verily I say unto you' (Ἀμὴν γὰρ λέγω ὑμῖν), to underscore the enduring authority and permanence of the Law. He states that 'one jot or one tittle' (ἰῶτα ἓν ἢ μία κεραία)—the smallest letter (iota) or a tiny stroke distinguishing similar letters in Hebrew—'shall in no wise pass from the law' (οὐ μὴ παρέλθῃ ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου) until 'all be fulfilled' (πάντα γένηται). This emphasizes the divine origin and complete fulfillment of every detail of God's word, ultimately in Christ (Luke 16:17).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse powerfully asserts the absolute and enduring authority of God's Word. It teaches that every detail of the Law and Prophets is divinely inspired and will be perfectly fulfilled, primarily through Christ's life, death, and resurrection, demonstrating God's faithfulness to His covenant promises.

verses.kjv_text: Whosoever therefore shall break one of these least commandments, and shall teach men so, he shall be called the least in the kingdom of heaven: but whosoever shall do and teach them, the same shall be called great in the kingdom of heaven.
verses.original_text: Ὃς ἐὰν οὖν λύσῃ μίαν τῶν ἐντολῶν τούτων τῶν ἐλαχίστων καὶ διδάξῃ οὕτως τοὺς ἀνθρώπους, ἐλάχιστος κληθήσεται ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τῶν οὐρανῶν· ὃς δ’ ἂν ποιήσῃ καὶ διδάξῃ, οὗτος μέγας κληθήσεται ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τῶν οὐρανῶν.
verses.transliteration: Hos ean oun lysē mian tōn entolōn toutōn tōn elachistōn kai didaxē houtōs tous anthrōpous, elachistos klēthēsetai en tē basileia tōn ouranōn; hos d' an poiēsē kai didaxē, houtos megas klēthēsetai en tē basileia tōn ouranōn.
verses.study_note: Jesus warns against 'break[ing] one of these least commandments' (λύσῃ μίαν τῶν ἐντολῶν τούτων τῶν ἐλαχίστων) and teaching others to do so, resulting in being 'called the least in the kingdom of heaven' (ἐλάχιστος κληθήσεται ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ τῶν οὐρανῶν). Conversely, 'whosoever shall do and teach them' (ὃς δ’ ἂν ποιήσῃ καὶ διδάξῃ) will be 'called great' (μέγας κληθήσεται). This highlights the importance of both obedience (doing) and faithful instruction (teaching) concerning God's commands, emphasizing the ethical demands of kingdom citizenship (James 2:10).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse underscores the importance of both obedience and faithful teaching of God's commands for kingdom standing. It teaches that true greatness in God's kingdom is measured by one's commitment to upholding and living out even the 'least' of God's precepts, demonstrating the integral link between faith and works.

verses.kjv_text: For I say unto you, That except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of heaven.
verses.original_text: Λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐὰν μὴ περισσεύσῃ ὑμῶν ἡ δικαιοσύνη πλεῖον τῶν γραμματέων καὶ Φαρισαίων, οὐ μὴ εἰσέλθητε εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τῶν οὐρανῶν.
verses.transliteration: Legō gar humin hoti ean mē perisseusē humōn hē dikaiosynē pleion tōn grammateōn kai Pharisaiōn, ou mē eiselthēte eis tēn basileia tōn ouranōn.
verses.study_note: Jesus makes a startling declaration: 'except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of heaven' (ἐὰν μὴ περισσεύσῃ ὑμῶν ἡ δικαιοσύνη πλεῖον τῶν γραμματέων καὶ Φαρισαίων, οὐ μὴ εἰσέλθητε εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τῶν οὐρανῶν). The scribes and Pharisees were known for their meticulous adherence to the Law. Jesus is not advocating for more external legalism, but for a deeper, internal righteousness of the heart, which is the true fulfillment of the Law (Rom 9:30-32).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse sets a radical standard for entry into the Kingdom of Heaven, demanding a righteousness that surpasses the external legalism of the Pharisees. It teaches that true righteousness is not merely outward conformity but an internal transformation of the heart, achievable only through Christ and His indwelling Spirit, which is foundational for salvation.

verses.kjv_text: Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not kill; and whosoever shall kill shall be in danger of the judgment:
verses.original_text: Ἠκούσατε ὅτι ἐρρέθη τοῖς ἀρχαίοις, Οὐ φονεύσεις· ὃς δ’ ἂν φονεύσῃ, ἔνοχος ἔσται τῇ κρίσει.
verses.transliteration: Ēkousate hoti errhethē tois archaiois, Ou phoneuseis; hos d' an phoneusē, enochos estai tē krisei.
verses.study_note: This is the first of six 'antitheses' where Jesus contrasts traditional interpretations of the Law with his own deeper understanding. He begins with the sixth commandment, 'Thou shalt not kill' (Οὐ φονεύσεις) (Exod 20:13; Deut 5:17). The traditional understanding focused on the physical act of murder, with the consequence being 'in danger of the judgment' (ἔνοχος ἔσται τῇ κρίσει), referring to local courts. Jesus is about to reveal the internal dimension of this command.

verses.kjv_text: But I say unto you, That whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment: and whosoever shall say to his brother, Raca, shall be in danger of the council: but whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire.
verses.original_text: Ἐγὼ δὲ λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι πᾶς ὁ ὀργιζόμενος τῷ ἀδελφῷ αὐτοῦ εἰκῇ ἔνοχος ἔσται τῇ κρίσει· ὃς δ’ ἂν εἴπῃ τῷ ἀδελφῷ αὐτοῦ, Ῥακά, ἔνοχος ἔσται τῷ συνεδρίῳ· ὃς δ’ ἂν εἴπῃ, Μωρέ, ἔνοχος ἔσται εἰς τὴν γέενναν τοῦ πυρός.
verses.transliteration: Egō de legō humin hoti pas ho orgizomenos tō adelphō autou eikē enochos estai tē krisei; hos d' an eipē tō adelphō autou, Rhaka, enochos estai tō synedriō; hos d' an eipē, Mōre, enochos estai eis tēn geennan tou pyros.
verses.study_note: Jesus radically redefines murder to include internal anger. 'Whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause' (πᾶς ὁ ὀργιζόμενος τῷ ἀδελφῷ αὐτοῦ εἰκῇ) is 'in danger of the judgment' (ἔνοχος ἔσται τῇ κρίσει). Using contemptuous terms like 'Raca' (Ῥακά, an Aramaic insult meaning 'empty-headed') puts one 'in danger of the council' (τῷ συνεδρίῳ, the Sanhedrin).
